sections.title: Overview
sections.content: External jugular access is an alternative venous route when peripheral IV access is difficult or has failed. It can be useful in selected critically unwell patients, but it should be balanced against airway access, cervical spine concerns, and the possibility that IO access may be faster and more reliable.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Position the patient to optimise vein filling if this does not worsen airway or spinal management

2. Turn the head only as much as is safe and clinically acceptable

3. Identify and distend the vein, then prepare the skin aseptically

4. Cannulate the vein using a suitable IV cannula and confirm free flow and flush

5. Secure the line carefully because neck movement can dislodge it easily

6. Reassess function after patient movement or repositioning

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- EJ access is often a short-term bridge rather than the ideal definitive line
- IO access is often preferable when rapid access for resuscitation is needed
- Be cautious if airway intervention, cervical collars, or spinal motion restriction limit access or make the line unsafe
- Document why EJ access was chosen and how well it functioned
